growth_records.cite: Madin JS et al. A synthesis of bacterial and archaeal phenotypic trait data. Sci Data 7:170 (2020); source dataset: engqvist.
growth_records.notes: Species-level trait record; culture mode/pH often unspecified; medium + uptake/secretion pending literature curation.

up.method: MIMS, Michaelis-Menten Vmax; Km(app)=3.5 μM CH4
MIMS, Vmax after H2 pulses (accelerated from 0.64 to 14.2 μmol/min/gDW over 43 h)

growth_records.cite: Ida F. Peterse et al. Hydrogen Oxidation Benefits Alphaproteobacterial Methanotrophs Under Severe Methane Limitation. Environmental Microbiology (2025). DOI:10.1111/1462-2920.70163. [PMC12319191]
growth_records.snippet: pre-grown in batch culture without CH4 limitation (μ = 0.065 h−1). This resulted in a maximum CH4 oxidation rate of 159 μmol min−1 g−1 DW with a Km(app) of 3.5 μM CH4. The maximum oxidation rate of H2 gradually increased ... from 0.64 to 14
growth_records.notes: CH4-replete batch reference condition (Table 1, μ=0.065 h−1). Vmax CH4=159 μmol/min/gDW; converted: 159*0.06=9.54 mmol/gDW/h. Vmax H2 max=14.2 μmol/min/gDW; 14.2*0.06=0.852 mmol/gDW/h (Figure S3). Km(app) CH4=3.5 μM. doubling_time=ln2/μ=0.6931/0.065=10.66 h. Rates are MEASURED max oxidation (=uptake) capacities via membrane-inlet mass spectrometry, not steady-state fluxes. Metagenome of bioreactor lineage contained a Variovorax contaminant (4% reads) but with NO hydrogenase genes.

up.method: MIMS Vmax, accelerated from 0.48 to 1.20 mmol/gDW/h (8→20 μmol/min/gDW); Km(app)=0.03-0.06 μM
steady-state net uptake qCH4=D/Yxs (with H2 co-feed)

yields.Yxs: 6.9 g DW/mol CH4 (no H2); 14 g DW/mol CH4 (with H2), adapted NTA medium
yields.product_yields: CH4-to-CO2 conversion 55%±1% (no H2) dropping to 13%±0.4% (with H2), i.e. carbon incorporation from CH4 into biomass rose from 45% to 87%

growth_records.cite: Ida F. Peterse et al. Hydrogen Oxidation Benefits Alphaproteobacterial Methanotrophs Under Severe Methane Limitation. Environmental Microbiology (2025). DOI:10.1111/1462-2920.70163. [PMC12319191]
growth_records.snippet: the effect of H2 on the growth yield of a purified M. bryophila H2sT culture (D = 0.006 h−1) was reassessed... the conversion of CH4 to CO2 dropped from 55% ± 1% to 13% ± 0.4%... corresponding to a yield increase from 6.9 to 14 g DW mol−1 C
growth_records.notes: CH4-limited chemostat, D=0.006 h−1; μ=D=0.006 at steady state; td=ln2/0.006=115.5 h. Table 1: Vmax CH4=155 μmol/min/gDW->155*0.06=9.30 mmol/gDW/h; Vmax H2 8->20 μmol/min/gDW ->0.48->1.20 mmol/gDW/h. Km(app) CH4=3 μM, H2=0.03-0.06 μM. Yields (reassessed, adapted medium): 6.9 (no H2), 14 (with H2) g DW/mol CH4. qCH4=D/Y: 0.006/6.9=8.70e-4 mol/gDW/h=0.87 mmol/gDW/h (no H2); 0.006/14=4.29e-4=0.43 mmol/gDW/h (with H2). qCO2 from stated CH4->CO2 conversion: 0.55*0.87=0.48 (no H2); 0.13*0.43=0.056 (with H2). OD600=1 = 247.5±4.6 mg DW/L at this D. Under stable H2-replete conditions total H2 consumption was 3x the CH4 consumption. All CO2 in outflow was culture-produced (no CO2 fed).

growth_records.cite: Ida F. Peterse et al. Hydrogen Oxidation Benefits Alphaproteobacterial Methanotrophs Under Severe Methane Limitation. Environmental Microbiology (2025). DOI:10.1111/1462-2920.70163. [PMC12319191]
growth_records.snippet: When 0.7 μM, 1.4 μM, and 2 μM H2S were added to the MIMS cell containing the M. bryophila H2sT culture (D = 0.003 h−1), the CH4 oxidation rate was inhibited by 48%, 74%, and 85%, respectively
growth_records.notes: CH4-limited chemostat D=0.003 h−1; μ=D=0.003; td=ln2/0.003=231 h. Table 1: Vmax CH4=84 μmol/min/gDW ->84*0.06=5.04 mmol/gDW/h; Km(app) CH4=2.5-3 μM; Vmax H2 and Km H2 = n.d. at this D. H2S dose-response on CH4 oxidation: 48%/74%/85% inhibition at 0.7/1.4/2 μM H2S. Methanol respiration (O2 uptake) only 66% inhibited at 9.5 μM H2S; H2 oxidation only ~50% inhibited (5->2.3 μmol/min/gDW) at 20 μM H2S. No dry weight determined at this D; authors used mean of D=0.0016 and 0.006 (271.6 mg DW/L).

up.method: MIMS Vmax, accelerated from 0.24 to 1.44 mmol/gDW/h (4→24 μmol/min/gDW); Km(app)=0.15-0.20 μM
steady-state net uptake qCH4=D/Yxs (with H2 co-feed)

yields.Yxs: 5.8 g DW/mol CH4 (no H2); 13.1 g DW/mol CH4 (with H2); 7.5 g DW/mol CH4 (after H2 removed)
yields.product_yields: total H2 consumption exceeded CH4 consumption by factor 6-7 under stable H2-replete conditions

growth_records.cite: Ida F. Peterse et al. Hydrogen Oxidation Benefits Alphaproteobacterial Methanotrophs Under Severe Methane Limitation. Environmental Microbiology (2025). DOI:10.1111/1462-2920.70163. [PMC12319191]
growth_records.snippet: The calculated yield of the culture without H2 compared to with H2 supply increased from 5.8 to 13.1 g DW mol−1 CH4... after H2 was again omitted... the newly calculated yield of M. bryophila H2sT on CH4 alone was 7.5 g DW mol−1 CH4
growth_records.notes: Severe CH4-limited chemostat D=0.0016 h−1 (dissolved CH4 0.11 μM); μ=D; td=ln2/0.0016=433 h. Table 1: Vmax CH4=61 μmol/min/gDW ->3.66 mmol/gDW/h; Vmax H2 4->24 μmol/min/gDW ->0.24->1.44 mmol/gDW/h; Km CH4=1-2 μM, Km H2=0.15-0.20 μM. Km/H2 kinetics: Km(app) H2/D2=35±5 nM without CH4, rising to 55 nM on CH4 addition (D2 consumption 12.6->17.4 μmol/min/gDW). Yields: 5.8 (no H2), 13.1 (with H2), 7.5 (H2 removed) g DW/mol CH4; note early low value attributed to trace-element limitation. qCH4=D/Y: 0.0016/5.8=2.76e-4=0.28 mmol/gDW/h (no H2); 0.0016/13.1=1.22e-4=0.12 mmol/gDW/h (with H2). OD600=1 = 295.8±4.7 mg DW/L at this D.
